1: Psalm 122 is the arrival psalm — the pilgrim has reached Jerusalem. Attributed to David, it reflects the perspective of a worshipper who has heard the summons and is now standing inside the city. "I was filled with joy (samahti) when they said to me, 'Let us go to the house of the LORD!'" — the invitation to go was itself the occasion of joy, not merely the arrival. In the honor-shame culture of ancient Israel, inclusion in a pilgrimage group was a form of social recognition: to be invited by "they" (the community) to go together to the temple was to be acknowledged as a member of the covenant community. The pilgrimage grammar is communal ("let us go"), not solitary; the joy is triggered by the communal summons, not by private devotion.
2: "Our feet are standing (hayu omdot) inside your gates, O Jerusalem" — the perfect tense with present force: "have taken their stand." The shift from "I" in v1 to "our feet" in v2 marks the transition from individual invitation to communal arrival — the whole pilgrimage company has reached the destination together. The gates of Jerusalem (she'arech) were the legal, commercial, and social thresholds of the covenant city; to stand inside them is to be within the city's protection and jurisdiction. For pilgrims who had traveled days through the Judean wilderness, this moment of standing inside Jerusalem's gates was the physical culmination of the journey. The Songs of Ascents were designed to be sung at precisely this threshold moment — from the outside looking in (Ps 120) to the inside standing in gratitude (Ps 122).
3: "Jerusalem — built as a city that is closely joined together (shehubberah-lah yahdav)" — the phrase is dense. Habar (to join, to connect, to be united) in the passive participle: the city built as a unit, joined closely to itself. This describes: (a) the physical compactness of Jerusalem's construction — the dense, stone-built city where no space was wasted and every wall connected to the next; (b) the social and spiritual unity of the people gathered there — "joined together" in covenant fellowship; (c) the theological joining of earth and heaven at the point where YHWH dwells. The pilgrims who came scattered from twelve tribal territories found in Jerusalem a city that was constitutively unified — the architectural expression of the covenant community's unity under one God.
4: "To which the tribes make their pilgrimage (shevatim alu) — the tribes of the LORD (shivte-yah) — as it was commanded of Israel, to give thanks to the name of the LORD" — the three great pilgrimage festivals (Exodus 23:14-17; Deuteronomy 16:16) required every male Israelite to "appear before the LORD" at the central sanctuary. Shivte-yah (tribes of the LORD) — the possessive suffix on the divine name is unusual and emphatic: these are not merely Israelite tribes but YHWH's own tribes, his covenant possession. The pilgrimage is both obligation ("as it was commanded") and identity expression: the tribes go up because this is who they are — YHWH's people returning to their God's house. The Feast of Tabernacles had an eschatological dimension; Zechariah 14:16-19 envisions all nations eventually ascending to Jerusalem for Sukkot.
5: "There the thrones of judgment stand (kisseot lemishpat) — the thrones belonging to the house of David" — the administrative-judicial center of Israel was at Jerusalem. Covenant law required difficult legal cases to be brought to the central sanctuary for adjudication (Deuteronomy 17:8-13: "go to the Levitical priests and to the judge who is in office at that time; they will give you the verdict"). The "thrones of the house of David" represent the royal judicial function — the king's responsibility to administer justice, particularly for the poor and powerless (Psalms 72:1-4; Isaiah 9:7; Psalms 11:4; Jeremiah 22:3, 15-16). The pilgrimage to Jerusalem was simultaneously access to the highest human court of appeal in Israel: justice and worship were not separate categories at Zion. The pilgrim went up to pray and to access covenant law.
6: "Pray for the peace of Jerusalem (shaalu shlom yerushalayim)! May all who love this city flourish (yishlayyu ohavayik)" — the wordplay between Yerushalayim (Jerusalem) and shalom (peace) is almost certainly intentional; the city's name contains the very thing being prayed for. The popular etymology (debated academically) connects Jerusalem to shalem (wholeness, completeness) from which shalom derives. To pray for Jerusalem's peace is to pray for what is written into the city's name — for her to become what she is called to be. Shaál (to ask, inquire, petition) suggests formal prayer, not merely a wish; the pilgrimage community is being instructed in an intercession practice that continued throughout Israel's history and into the synagogue's daily prayers (the Amidah's 14th benediction prays for Jerusalem's restoration).
7: "May there be peace within your walls (bechélech shalom) and safety (shalvah) within your strongholds (be'armenotayik)" — the two layers of the city's defensive architecture: the outer wall rampart (hel) and the inner palatial fortresses (armon). Peace within walls and security within strongholds represents both the political stability of the city and the social ordering of its internal life. The pilgrim who just arrived (v2) now prays for the city's sustained wellbeing — not merely as a visitor benefiting from its peace but as a covenant community member whose flourishing is bound up with Jerusalem's flourishing. Jeremiah 29:7 ("seek the peace of the city where I have sent you into exile") extended this prayer practice even to Babylon: the covenant community prays for the peace of whatever city it inhabits.
8: "For the sake of my brothers and friends (ahai ve're'ai) I say, 'May peace be with you!'" — the pilgrimage city is where the covenant network of brothers and friends is located. In the ancient world, "brothers" and "friends" were not merely biological or personal categories but represented the covenant kinship network — those with whom one had binding obligations of loyalty and mutual aid. The three great festivals gathered the dispersed community in one place, maintaining the social bonds that geographic distance would otherwise dissolve. To pray for Jerusalem's peace for the sake of brothers and friends is to pray for the social fabric that makes covenant community possible: the network of mutual obligation, shared worship, and reciprocal loyalty that the pilgrimage system was designed to sustain.
9: "For the sake of the house of the LORD our God (beit-YHWH eloheinu) I will work for your wellbeing (avakshah tov lak)" — the ultimate motivation for Jerusalem's wellbeing is theological, not merely social. The house of YHWH (beit-YHWH) is the temple, the covenant dwelling-point where heaven and earth intersect. To seek Jerusalem's good "for the sake of the house of the LORD" is to recognize that the city's welfare is inseparable from the welfare of worship. The practical commitment (avakesh, "I will seek, actively pursue, work toward") goes beyond prayer into action — the pilgrim becomes an agent of the city's wellbeing upon returning home, because the house of YHWH that stands in it is the source of all blessing that flows outward to the covenant community and from there to the nations (Psalms 67:1-2; Psalms 96:3).
